coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TC) is a common antibiotic that poses risks to human and animal health when present in samples. pH is a crucial parameter for assessing water quality and food safety. The innovative ratiometric probe, designed based on dual-emissive phosphorus-doped carbon dots (DE-P-CDs), was synthesized through a green hydrothermal process. The sensor exhibited high analytical performance toward TC detection with a LOD of 5.78 μM and 6.18 μM, respectively, and visual pH detection. Furthermore, the DE-P-CDs were employed for visual pH sensing, with the solution color smoothly changing with variations in pH. The color transitioned from red to orange, green to dark green, and blue to dark blue, facilitating easy visual observation. This probe proves to be a versatile tool for detecting TC in milk and tap water samples, with visual capabilities for pH. Two metric tools which were AGREE and BAGI were also used to evaluate the method's greenness. The evaluation affirmed its outstanding environmental friendliness. It represents a valuable contribution to the fields of analytical chemistry, food, and environmental monitoring.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline (TC) is a common antibiotic that poses risks to human and animal health when present in samples. pH is a crucial parameter for assessing water quality and food safety. The innovative ratiometric probe, designed based on dual-emissive phosphorus-doped carbon dots (DE-P-CDs), was synthesized through a green hydrothermal process. The sensor exhibited high analytical performance toward TC detection with a LOD of 5.78 μM and 6.18 μM, respectively, and visual pH detection. Furthermore, the DE-P-CDs were employed for visual pH sensing, with the solution color smoothly changing with variations in pH. The color transitioned from red to orange, green to dark green, and blue to dark blue, facilitating easy visual observation. This probe proves to be a versatile tool for detecting TC in milk and tap water samples, with visual capabilities for pH. Two metric tools which were AGREE and BAGI were also used to evaluate the method's greenness. The evaluation affirmed its outstanding environmental friendliness. It represents a valuable contribution to the fields of analytical chemistry, food, and environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: TC is a widely used antibiotic with broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, cost-effectiveness, and growth-promoting effects in human and animal medicine, as well as in the food industry (Shatery et al., 2024; Dai et al., 2020; Xigui Liu et al., 2018). However, its extensive use in veterinary treatment has led to its detection in various food products, such as dairy, honey, meat, and chicken, and its release into the environment due to its slow metabolism and limited absorption (B. Shi et al., 2023; Wong et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2021). These pose serious threats to food safety and environmental health, as TC can cause antibiotic resistance, allergic reactions, and toxicity in humans and animals. Therefore, it is imperative to develop effective methods for monitoring TC levels in complex food matrices and water samples (J. Wang et al., 2018; Khezerlou et al., 2023).

Among the various analytical techniques that have been applied for TC detection, such as electrochemical analysis (Kim et al., 2010), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Vargas, & CeciliaReyesRath, 2009), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (G. Mu et al., 2012), and chemiluminescence method (Townshend et al., 2005), Fluorescence (FL) technique has attracted considerable attention because of its cost-effectiveness, heightened sensitivity, rapid response, and straightforward operation (Xinfang Liu et al., 2021; Linli Hou, 2019).

Moreover, the FL technique can also be used for pH sensing, which is of great importance for monitoring various biological processes and environmental conditions. However, most of the fluorescent probes used for TC detection and pH sensing are based on single emission, which can be affected by external factors, such as excitation light intensity, environmental interference, and probe concentration (L. Shi et al., 2021). These factors can cause variations in FL intensity and reduce the accuracy and reliability of the measurements (Bigdeli et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2019).

To overcome these limitations, ratiometric fluorescent probes have been developed, which can provide self-calibration by using two emission peaks with different responses to the analyte of interest. Ratiometric fluorescent probes can also enable visual detection by exhibiting color changes under a UV lamp, which can improve the convenience and efficiency of the analysis (Kayani & Chalak, 2024; S. Mu et al., 2021; Y. Wang et al., 2016; Yarur et al., 2019; Han et al., 2020) However, most of the existing ratiometric systems are either composed of mixed components or require long reaction times, which can compromise their stability and applicability. Therefore, there is a need for novel ratiometric fluorescent probes that can achieve rapid and selective detection of TC and pH with high stability and simplicity (B. Li et al., 2019).

In recent years, various fluorescent nanomaterials (Roostaie, Haddad, and Haji Abdolrasouli 2024; Zhu et al., 2023) have been explored for the detection of TC, such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) (Khataee et al., 2020; Shatery et al., 2024), quantum dots (QDs) (Anand et al., 2019), and nanoclusters (NCs) (Song et al., 2020; Yuanyuan Li et al., 2020). However, these nanomaterials have some drawbacks that limit their practical applications. For instance, MOFs often suffer from poor chemical selectivity (Kreno et al., 2012), QDs have potential toxicity due to their metal ions (Mishra et al., 2018), and NCs tend to have low stability (W. Yan et al., 2020). Therefore, there is a demand for novel fluorescent nanomaterials that can overcome these challenges and offer high performance for TC detection.

Phosphorous-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) are a type of carbon dots (CDs), which are fluorescent nanomaterials smaller than 10 nm (Zuo et al., 2020). CDs have many advantages, such as simple synthesis (Kayani and Abdullah 2024; Kayani, Rahim, et al., 2024), high water solubility (Ehtesabi et al., 2019), low toxicity (Campos et al., 2016; Cui et al., 2017; Fan et al., 2018; Su et al., 2022), and excellent biocompatibility (J. Hou, 2019), and they have been used for various purposes, such as bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, catalysis, and agriculture (Ji et al., 2020; Khan & Patil, 2020; F. Yan et al., 2018). However, P-CDs are even more superior, as they have enhanced fluorescence, stability, solubility, and biocompatibility, due to the presence of phosphorus atoms in their carbon structure (Ying Li et al., 2017). P-CDs can be easily produced by various methods and used for sensing, imaging, catalysis, and energy conversion (Tadesse et al., 2021). P-CDs are therefore a very important and promising type of nanomaterial (Zhi et al., 2018).

One of the challenges in the development of CDs is the synthesis of red-emitting CDs, which are rare in the literature and often have low quantum yields, complex synthesis processes, and high cost. However, red-emitting CDs have more advantages than blue- or green-emitting CDs, such as higher penetration depth, lower background interference, and better photostability (Pawar et al., 2020). Therefore, it is desirable and challenging to develop an effective and simple method for preparing red-emitting CDs with high quantum yield and stability (Miaoran Zhang, Wu, et al., 2019).

Smartphones are convenient and easy-to-use devices that have gained widespread popularity in recent years. They have been applied in various domains for on-site analysis, using their built-in cameras as optical sensors to measure the concentrations of different analytes. The smartphone camera has several advantages for this task, such as high-resolution image capture, manual or automatic exposure and focus control, and efficient data storage (Li Hou, 2019; W. Li et al., 2021; H. Wang, Peng, et al., 2019). Moreover, smartphones can be combined with paper-based substrates, which are cost-effective and suitable for on-the-spot analysis, mobility, and versatile identification of substances. The integration of smartphone detection with fluorescent test paper offers even more possibilities for sensing applications (Kayani & Omer, 2022).

In this study, we present the synthesis of a novel type of DE-P-CDs as a ratiometric probe for TC detection and pH sensing. The synthesis of DE-P-CDs is achieved by a simple and facile one-step hydrothermal method, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . The DE-P-CDs exhibit two distinct emission peaks at 486 nm and 605 nm under 400 nm excitation, which can be influenced by TC and pH, respectively. The FL intensity ratio of the two emission peaks decreases linearly with increasing TC concentration, while the FL intensity ratio shifts linearly with changing pH values. The DE-P-CDs also show obvious color changes under a UV lamp, which can enable the visual detection of TC and pH. We have developed an effective visual pH detection technique by leveraging the unique properties of these DE-P-CDs. Moreover, data analysis is based on the ratio of FL intensities from the DE-P-CDs and the RGB values captured using a smartphone camera. This probe can sense both TC and pH with high speed, accuracy, and efficiency. It requires no expensive or complicated devices, making it suitable for food and environmental quality control.

Cellulose, orthophosphoric acid (H3PO4), Manganese Chloride (MnCl2), Cobalt chloride (CoCl2), aluminum (III) nitrate Al(NO3)3, iron (II) nitrate Fe(NO3)3, copper chloride (CuCl2), as well as any metal salts, ascorbic acid, glucose, lactose, phenylalanine, aspartic acid, were purchased from commercial suppliers (Merck and Sigma-Aldrich). The research laboratory utilized for our study was outfitted with a Molecular Weight Cutoff (MWCO) membrane and a variety of chemical compounds.

The fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were documented on a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) and a Cary 60 Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA), respectively. A diamond ATR attachment was used to detect the functional groups of D-PCDs using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer, the FTIR Nicolet iS50 (Thermo Scientific, USA). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained by FEI Tecnai G2 F30. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained on a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi.

The DE-P-CDs were synthesized through the following steps. Initially, 0.25 g of cellulose were dissolved in 20 mL of 2.5 M H3PO4. Subsequently, the resulting solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 1.5 h. After cooling, the obtained solution underwent filtration through a 0.22 μm filter, followed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove larger particles. Finally, the solution was dialyzed against deionized water to ensure the removal of any residual impurities and unreacted reagents.

TC was dissolved in distilled water to make a tetracycline standard solution with a specific concentration. Generally, 1 mL of DE-P-CDs with different concentrations of tetracycline was added to the pH of the solution at normal (pH = 3), and then diluted to 4 mL by DW for further analytical use. The probe's FL emission spectra were documented at different concentrations.

First, the pH was measured at various pH level in a range of pH 1 to 12. 0.3 mL DE-P-CDs were diluted to 3 mL with water and NaOH or HCl. The different pH values were prepared in a range of pH 1 to 12, the FL of the solutions under UV lamp was taken for each pH solutions by smartphone camera. The RGB data of the photos were analyzed by Image-J software.

The food samples were prepared following the procedure below. The milk samples were acquired from a local store, so as to eliminate the fats and proteins, several procedures were achieved by a literature (Han et al., 2020), To begin, 200 μL (10% v/v) of trichloroacetic acid was added to the 2 mL of milk samples, and the mixture was sonicated for 20 min. After that the solution was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm. Finally, the precipitate was removed by filtering the resultant supernatant through a 0.22 μm membrane. The samples of water were measured according to previous study (Jia et al., 2021). In the laboratory, tap water was obtained directly from the pipe without any unnecessary purification or treatment. TC standard samples of various concentrations were prepared and set away using tap water.

The quantum yield (QY) of the DE-P-CDs is determined by comparing the integrated FL intensities (excited at 400 nm) and the absorbance values (at 400 nm) of the DE-P-CDs with fluorescein as a reference. Fluorescein, with a QY of 0.95, is dissolved in 0.1 M NaOH (refractive index of 1.33), while the DE-P-CDs are dissolved in water (refractive index of 1.33).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, formation mechanism, and characterization of DE-P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The DE-P-CDs were synthesized through an environmentally friendly hydrothermal process, utilizing cellulose and phosphoric acid as the carbon and phosphorus sources, respectively. Although previous literature has documented the hydrothermal synthesis of phosphorous carbon dots from small organic compounds, carbohydrates, and phosphoric acid, a comprehensive and explicit mechanism for this process remains absent.

To address this gap, we propose a detailed mechanism for the formation of DE-P-CDs from cellulose and phosphoric acid, based on existing literature on the carbonization of carbohydrates and small organic compounds, as depicted in Scheme 2 . The synthesis of DE-P-CDs involves several sequential steps. In the initial step, cellulose undergoes hydrolysis when treated with phosphoric acid and water, breaking down the β-1,4-glycosidic bonds to produce glucose molecules (A) (Huang & Yao, 2013). Subsequently, glucose molecules undergo dehydration, forming hydroxymethyl furfural (B), a five-carbon sugar derivative (De & Karak, 2013; Dee & Bell, 2011), In the third step, hydroxymethyl furfural (B) reacts with phosphoric acid and water, leading to the formation of levulinic acid (C) and formic acid (D), which serve as the acid sources (González-Domínguez et al., 2018). The fourth step involves the formation of a hydrogen phosphate compound (E) through the phosphate ester bond, acting as the phosphate source (González-Domínguez et al., 2018). Finally, in the last step, the compounds (A, B, C, D, and E) undergo a series of reactions, including self-condensation, aldol condensation, and hydrogen bond interactions (Mohammed et al., 2024; Mohammed et al., 2023). Following the completion of aromatization/carbonization, the DE-P-CDs are formed as the final product (W. Wang, Peng, et al., 2019; Kalaiyarasan et al., 2020), exhibiting diverse functional groups on their surface that contribute to their dual-emissive behavior.

This proposed mechanism aims to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the synthesis process for DE-P-CDs, bridging the gap in the existing literature regarding the formation of phosphorus-doped carbon dots through hydrothermal methods. Several methods were utilized for the examination of the synthesized DE-P-CDs, including FTIR, XPS, UV–Vis spectroscopy, PL spectroscopy, HR-TEM, and XRD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological studies of DE-P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups on the surface of DE-P-CDs were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 1 A shows the FTIR spectrum of DE-P-CDs, which have distinct peaks corresponding to different functional groups. The broad peak in the range of 3600–2500 cm−1 indicates the presence of hydroxyl groups (O–H) of alcohol and carboxylic acid, which can act as hydrogen bond donors and enhance the solubility and stability of the DE-P-CDs (Kader et al., 2024; Mohammed et al., 2023). The peak at 2359 cm−1 is attributed to phosphine groups (P–H), playing a crucial role in phosphorus doping and conferring dual-emission properties to the carbon dots (Venkateswara Raju et al., 2020). The peak at 1697 cm−1 corresponds to carbonyl groups (CO) of carboxylic acid (Zhi et al., 2018), while the peak at 1636 cm−1 is assigned to carbon-carbon double bonds (CC) of the graphene structure (Egorova et al., 2023; Zhong et al., 2018), which are the main structural units of the DE-P-CDs. The peak at 1139 cm−1 is related to carbon-oxygen single bonds (C–O) (Zhao et al., 2019). Two peaks at 1004 cm−1 and 889 cm−1 are associated with phosphorus-oxygen bonds PO and P–O, respectively (Kalaiyarasan et al., 2020). These peaks are the main signs of phosphorus doping of the DE-P-CDs. The FTIR spectrum reveals that the DE-P-CDs have rich and diverse functional groups on the surface.

The XRD pattern of DE-P-CDs, as depicted in Fig. 1B, displays a broad peak at around 22.75°. This peak signifies the existence of graphitic carbon (001) crystal planes within the DE-P-CDs (Jelinek, 2017; Papaioannou et al., 2018).

The size, shape, and morphology of the DE-P-CDs were assessed by HRTEM. Fig. 2 C and D show the HRTEM images and particle size distribution of the DE-P-CDs, which have a nearly spherical shape with an average diameter of 1.3 nm. The (002) lattice spacing distance, shown in Fig. 2D, is 0.35 nm, slightly larger than that of bulk graphite (0.336 nm) (Wu et al., 2018). This difference is attributed to the presence of phosphorus groups in the carbon core and functional groups on the surface of the DE-P-CDs.

XPS was also performed to investigate the surface state, bonding, and chemical composition of DE-P-CDs. Fig. 2A depicts the binding energies of C1s, O1s, and P2p, which were 285 eV, 533 eV, and 134 eV, respectively. These values confirm that carbon, oxygen, and phosphorus are the major elements of DE-P-CDs, indicating successful phosphorus doping. The high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2B shows four peaks at 284.8 eV (C–C/CC), 286.33 eV (C–O), 287.7 eV (CO), and 288.91 eV (C–P) (Bandi et al., 2018). Fig. 2C shows the high-resolution O1s spectra of DE-P-CDs, which have four peaks at 531.58 eV (CO), 532.6 eV (C–O), 533.53 eV (PO), and 534.49 eV (P–O). In Fig. 2D, the deconvoluted P (2p) peaks at 134.21 eV correspond to (P–C), while peaks at 134.9 eV can be attributed to (P–O) (Barman et al., 2021; Goswami et al., 2022). The XPS spectrum confirms that the DE-P-CDs have phosphorus doping and a rich and diverse surface functionality.

In Fig. 3 A, an excitation and emission spectrum were obtained. The excitation spectrum, acquired by monitoring the red emission at 605 nm, exhibits a similarity to the results presented in the absorption spectrum. The emitted spectra show two emissions at 486 nm and 605 nm under excitation at 400 nm. The UV–Vis spectrum of DE-P-CDs, shows emission peaks at 275 nm and two emission peaks in the range of 330–500 nm. The first absorption band could be attributed to π-π* transitions and between 330 and 500 nm which could be attributed to the n-π* transitions (related to surface functional groups) as shown in Fig. 3B (Davi et al., 2021; Uriarte et al., 2019). To explore the excitation wavelength dependence of DE-P-CDs in the 380–430 nm range. As shown in Fig. 3C the FL intensity of DE-P-CDs gradually at 486 nm decreased while at 605 nm increased from 390 to 410 nm. Under 400 nm light excitation, the best FL intensity was achieved with an emission peak at 486 and 605 nm. The stability of the synthesized DE-P-CDs was tested. The FL intensity did not decrease under an excitation wavelength of 400 nm for 2 months as shown in Fig. 3D, as shown in Fig. 3D the stability of synthesized DE-P-CDs was evaluated in this study and demonstrates relatively high stability, which is advantageous for biosensing and practical applications.

The QY of DE-P-CDs, synthesized in an aqueous solution at room temperature is measured to be 4.2%. This measurement is done using fluorescein as a reference, with 0.1 M NaOH serving as the solvent. It's important to highlight that the quantum yield of fluorescein is 0.95.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism between TC and DE-P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It was observed that the FL of DE-P-CDs might be suppressed selectively by tetracycline, demonstrating that the as-prepared DE-P-CDs can serve as a sensitive fluorescence sensor for TC detection to find out more about the probable quenching mechanism, as illustrated in Fig. 3A and B, the interaction between of TC with DE-P-CDs leads to the FL of DE-P-CDs decrease (inset photo) For further exploration, we compared the excitation spectrum and UV spectra of DE-P-CDs with the UV–visible absorption spectrum of TC. We found that the UV absorption spectrum of TC and the excitation spectrum of DE-P-CDs exhibited a significant spectral overlap. This overlap could reduce the intensity of FL emission from the probe, indicating that the sensing mechanism is inner filter effect (IFE).

The consistency of photoluminescence stability demonstrated by DE-P-CDs in aqueous environments is of considerable interest for making realistic claims. Various factors, including temperature and ionic concentration, have the potential to affect their performance. To investigate this, the research initially assessed the fluorescence emission intensity of DE-P-CDs in solutions across varying temperatures and salt concentrations. The impact of temperature on DE-P-CDs indicates no notable alteration in fluorescence intensity within the temperature range of 35–65 °C (Fig. 4 A), consistent with observations from prior studies. Fig. 4B depicts the fluorescence intensity of DE-P-CDs in the presence of NaCl. The synthesized DE-P-CDs exhibit stability within the NaCl concentration range of 0.1–1.3 M, rendering them well-suited for a variety of applications.

To evaluate the sensor's selectivity in the presence of other common metals and molecules, the fabricated sensor was tested. The method included preparing metal and molecule stock solutions at a concentration of 0.01 M, followed by the addition of 400 μL of the probe to achieve a total volume of 4 mL, leading to a final interference concentration of 1 mM. Subsequently, the FL intensity of the resulting mixtures was measured using an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The findings indicated that only tetracycline caused a quenching effect on the FL intensity of the sensor. As shown in Fig. 5 A. To determine the feasibility of utilizing the sensor for analyzing real samples, selectivity studies were conducted on C-dots for tetracycline. Metal ions including Hg2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, and Cr3+, along with molecules such as glucose (Glu), Arginine (Arg), tartaric acid (Ta), glycine (Gly), Histidine (His), ascorbic acid (AA), phenylalanine (Phy), cysteine (Cys), Trichloroacetic acid (TCA), and TC were chosen to investigate their influence on C-dots. Fig. 5B shows that the addition of the aforementioned metal ions and molecules to the C-dot solution did not result in any significant changes. Overall, the introduction of tetracycline effectively extinguished the fluorescence of DE-P-CDs, demonstrating their potential application for detecting TC in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra of DE-P-CDs in different pH
ce:section.ce:para: As is well known, pH is important in various biological systems, and an alkaline pH environment is associated with factors such as food safety, environmental quality, and other factors (Long et al., 2020). Fig. 7 shows the influence of pH on the emission peak of DE-P-CDs, the response of DE-P-CDs with a pH range of 1–12, and the FL peaks at 480 nm and 595 nm were changed. The results indicated that the DE-P-CDs exhibited significant pH-dependent behavior, as evidenced by the gradual shift in the emission peaks at 480 nm and 595 nm across the pH range from 1.0 to 12.0. The FL of the DE-P-CDs varied from red to blue (Fig. 7), and the apparent blue shift in the FL spectrum presented a good opportunity for ratio measurement. Specifically, the emission ratio (I480/I595) sharply decreased from 0.45 to 1.77 within the pH range of 1.0–12.0 (Fig. 6B). The sigmoidal fitting of the emission ratio (F480/F595) yielded a calculated pKa value of 6.0. Moreover, the emission ratio exhibited favorable linearity within the pH range of 5.0–9.0 (R2 = 0.9893). These findings demonstrate the potential of DE-P-CDs for quantitative determination of normal acidic and basic pH using both conventional fluorescence and ratiometric fluorescence methods. The color change of the solutions under a UV lamp (365 nm), proved the use of DE-P-CDs as a sensing platform for quantitative pH measurements in the range of pH from 1 to 12 without the usage of FL spectrometers.

The created FL sensors require the use of a FL spectrophotometer, which isn't readily available for on-site and quick visualization detection (Gan et al., 2021). In order to solve the problem, naked-eye detection is a simple and effective detection method in which the output findings may be discriminated against and observed without the usage of complicated equipment (Mengyao Zhang, & YeHeZhangPingQianWu, 2020; Kayani & Omer, 2022). The proposed sensor gives shifts in FL intensity ratio causing a series of distinct color changes that can be seen with the naked eye and used in point-of-care monitoring of pH. The aim is to induce visualization of pH values as a visual detection platform. Therefore, the different pH value of the DE-P-CDs was tested in real-time. The photo of the serious pH from 1 to 12 was captured by a smartphone camera under a UV lamp, by changing the pH from 1 to 7 the FL color of the solution changed from dark red to orange while the change from 8 to 11 led to fluorescence color shift from red to green then to blue with different RGB value was obtained by Image-J software. This probe provides obvious changes from pH 1 to 12 in the fluorescence color of the solutions. To evaluate this study shown in Fig. 8 . The DE-P-CDs show remarkable pH switching from 1 to 12. However, a precise pH test utilizing dual emissive carbon dots has not been achieved, the significant fluctuation in FL color in the solution could be used to estimate the general pH value with the naked eye. As a result, DE-P-CDs can serve as potential pH sensors in both deionized and tap water samples. As illustrated in Table 2 , both samples were used as analytical models. The results calculated by the proposed DE-P-CDs-based probe and those measured by the pH-meter are essentially the same.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in real samples of TC using in-solution FL
ce:section.ce:para: The TC samples were obtained from different sources (milk, and water). The recommended assay's efficiency was evaluated by using the spike recovery method. The recovery data of TC in the spiked samples of milk, and water were in the range of 87.55–104.40 %, and 92.93–96.10 %, respectively. The findings indicate that the DE-P-CDs could be used successfully to estimate TC in real samples. As shown in Table 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the method of greenness
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of chemical processes on the environment and health is currently a topic of great interest. It is essential to determine the environmental safety of the analytical procedures to eliminate any possible harm to the environment (Kayani, Shatery, et al., 2024; Osman et al., 2023). The environmental friendliness of the developed method was assessed using two metric tools: AGREE and BAGI.

Firstly, an objective evaluation of the proposed methodology's environmental impact is provided by a greenness metric software known as the Analytical Greenness Calculator (AGREE) assessment tool. The clock-shaped graph provided by AGREE delineates the 12 principles of green analytical chemistry across 12 separate segments. Each segment corresponds to a single principle and is labeled red, yellow, or green based on the degree to which the analytical process adheres to green principles. The overall assessment value, ranging from 0 to 1, is depicted in the center of the AGREE graph (Elnaggar et al., 2023). The AGREE tool has been employed to assess the two developed approaches. As depicted in Fig. 9 A, both methods exhibit environmentally friendly characteristics, with the smartphone-based method achieving a higher analytical greenness score compared to the fluorimetry method (scores for both methods are 0.85 and 0.81, respectively). This superiority is attributed to factors such as smaller sample size, the ability to analyze multiple samples, lower energy consumption, and miniaturization. These findings validate the safety, environmental friendliness, and eco-benign nature of the developed methods.

The introduction of the blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI) proposes a novel metric for evaluating the practicality of an analytical method. BAGI evaluates ten key attributes, including the type of analysis, simultaneous determination of analytes, sample analysis rate, reagents and materials used, required instrumentation, simultaneous sample treatment, preconcentration requirements, automation level, sample preparation method, and sample quantity (Manousi et al., 2023). For the first time, we employed the BAGI method to evaluate the environmental friendliness of the proposed methods based on fluorescent nanomaterials. As depicted in Fig. 9B, the pictograms of both methods indicate BAGI scores of 82.5 and 75, respectively, proving the smartphone-based method achieved a higher score than the fluorimetry method. Additionally, both methods demonstrate strong applicability for TC and pH analysis.

Dual-Emissive Phosphorus-Doped Carbon Dots were synthesized from cellulose and phosphoric acid using the hydrothermal technique for the first time, this sensor was designed for the detection of TC in milk and water, coupled with a pH sensor. The probe exhibited remarkable selectivity and sensitivity, additionally, the ability of the probe for visual detection of pH, and point-of-care testing is possible. When compared to standard single-emission systems, the new method performed extremely well analytically. This study was shown to have opened up a new approach for detecting TC in various real samples and visual pH monitoring.
